draft.introduction: Zhu Xi’s commentary on the Great Learning established Neo-Confucianism as the orthodox philosophical system of East Asia for seven centuries. His synthesis of Confucian ethics with cosmological metaphysics reshaped Chinese intellectual life.

translation.section: Commentary on the Great Learning, Chapter 1
translation.original_ref: Zhu Xi, Commentary on the Great Learning
translation.text: The Way of the Great Learning lies in illuminating luminous virtue, in renewing the people, and in resting in the highest good. The extension of knowledge lies in the investigation of things. When things are investigated, knowledge is extended; when knowledge is extended, the will becomes sincere.
translation.original_text: 大學之道，在明明德，在親民，在止於至善

translator_notes.note: Zhu Xi’s phrase “investigation of things” (ge wu) became the most debated concept in Neo-Confucianism. Wang Yangming later argued it meant investigating the mind itself, not external objects. This dispute—rationalism versus intuitionism—parallels Western epistemological debates.
